title: AI Imposter Syndrome for New Grads
description: Is using AI as a new grad a crutch or a jet pack? Either way it feels kind of weird.
contentMarkdown:
One of the stranger side effects of using AI as a new grad is that it can make your output improve faster than your self-concept.

You use it to write a cleaner first draft, debug something faster, explore a new stack, or ship a small project that would have taken you much longer on your own. On paper, that should feel empowering. Instead, it often creates a vague sense that the result is not fully yours. Like you got help in a way that makes your work less legitimate.

I think a lot of people are feeling this right now, even if they are not naming it directly.

The core mistake is confusing reduced friction with reduced legitimacy.

That confusion hits new grads especially hard. Early in your career, you are already trying to figure out whether you really know what you are doing. You have less stored confidence, less proof behind you, and less margin for feeling shaky. So when AI helps you do something that might have been out of reach a year ago, it is easy to interpret that as evidence that you are faking it.

But that interpretation points at the wrong problem.

The whole point of a tool is to help you do things you could not do as easily before.

That is not a weird exception in tech. It is the whole history of the field.

Nobody thinks you are a fake engineer because you use frameworks instead of writing everything from scratch. Nobody thinks your deployment is illegitimate because you used AWS instead of manually provisioning physical servers. Nobody argues that GPS is cheating because you did not memorize every road in a city before driving through it. Nobody treats calculators as moral failure because they let you do more than mental arithmetic.

We accept all of those forms of leverage pretty easily because they feel mechanical. AI feels different because it gets much closer to thought, authorship, and identity. It helps in places people are used to attaching their self-worth.

That is why it creates a sharper psychological reaction.

There is also a more social layer to this that people do not talk about enough.

Even in tech, the game is not purely about doing good work. It is also political. People are constantly sending signals about competence, independence, effort, taste, and trustworthiness. A lot of career behavior is not just about producing outcomes. It is about making your competence legible to other people.

That is part of why visible struggle still carries status. It looks like effort. It looks like depth. It looks earned.

AI complicates that. It makes it harder to tell, from the outside, how much someone really understands versus how much they were able to accelerate through tooling. So people get nervous. Not just internally, but socially. They start asking themselves questions like: if I use this too much, will people think I am shallow? Will they think I do not actually know what I am doing? Am I building real skill, or just learning how to look productive?

That fear is not irrational. It is responding to a real social environment.

But it still helps to be precise about what the actual risk is.

The risk is not that AI helped you.

The risk is that you stop vetting it/owning judgment.

If AI helps you move faster, explore more options, compress activation energy, and attempt work slightly above your old level, that is not evidence of fraud. That is leverage doing what leverage is supposed to do. The problem starts when you use it to avoid forming a view. When you ship things you cannot explain, accept answers you cannot verify, or let generated output substitute for understanding.

That is the real line.

Not: did AI touch the work?

But:
- Do you understand what you built?
- Can you verify it?
- Can you debug it when it breaks?
- Can you explain why one approach is better than another?
- Are you still making the decisions that matter?

That is the standard that actually matters. Not whether the path there looked difficult enough to satisfy someone else’s intuition about what effort should look like.

I think this is especially important for new grads because AI creates a tempting but fragile shortcut. It can let your output outrun your foundations. That is useful up to a point. In fact, some of that is good. A tool should expand your range. But if your range expands faster than your understanding and you never close the gap, eventually you will feel it. The confidence will be fake because it has nothing solid underneath it.

So the answer is not to avoid AI in order to preserve some older feeling of legitimacy.

The answer is to use AI while staying responsible for your understanding.

That means using it to reduce friction, not replace judgment. It means letting it help you get moving, compare options, structure messy information, and attempt things you would have struggled to do alone. But it also means checking whether you actually understand what happened afterward. Could you explain the tradeoffs to another person? Could you fix the thing if it broke in a slightly different way? Could you tell when the output was subtly wrong?

Those are much better questions than whether the work was “really yours.”

Because if you think about it honestly, a lot of engineering has never been about isolated raw effort. It has been about learning how to use abstraction, tooling, systems, and other people’s work to produce something useful. Libraries, docs, cloud services, Stack Overflow, frameworks, senior engineers, design systems, open source, templates. Tech has always rewarded people who can use leverage well, and this creates a huge opportunity for new grads that aren’t pigeonholed into old ways of doing things.

And because it gets close to thinking, it also makes people more self-conscious. It threatens the old prestige model where visible effort stood in for legitimacy. If a new tool helps you do more, faster, and with less friction, some part of you may feel like you have skipped a rite of passage. Maybe you did skip some friction. Good. That is what tools are for.

The important thing is not to worship friction just because previous generations paid it.

New grads do not need less leverage. They need stronger ownership.

That means you should absolutely use AI to raise the ceiling of what you can attempt. You should use it to write, build, debug, research, and unblock yourself. You should let it make you more ambitious. But you should also be careful not to confuse acceleration with understanding. If you keep your judgment in the loop, AI is not making you a fraud. It is making you more capable.

So I do not think AI imposter syndrome is entirely fake. It is pointing at something real. But it is usually pointing at the wrong thing.

The real problem is not that AI made the work easier.

It is whether you gave up ownership of the thinking that matters.
